results.possibleIdentity: Paul Sentner is a photographer, artist, and art dealer who has been active since the late 1960s. In 1967, he served as a VISTA (Volunteers in Service to America) volunteer in New York City, where he worked in the Southeast Bronx and the Lower East Side. During this period, he documented urban community life and slum housing conditions through photography, with his work featured in the 'VISTA Volunteer' journal. His involvement in the New York art and social activist scene of the late 1960s aligns with the timeframe and mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which frequently collaborated with community and social service groups in the same neighborhoods. By the 1980s and into the 2000s, Sentner was based in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, where he became known as a local historian of the Elliott and West End neighborhoods and continued his work as a 'sometime photographer and art dealer.' His interests have spanned the intersection of art and technology, evidenced by his contributions to technical newsletters (such as 'Applying The Atari') where he shared designs for software intended to assist visually impaired individuals in reading computer displays.

results.possibleIdentity: John Harris (born 1937) is an American artist who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, specifically recognized for his involvement with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He frequently collaborated with Fred Stern (born 1936), an artist and engineer. Their collaborative project, titled "K.E.P.-1 Self" (1968), was included in the influential E.A.T. exhibition "Some More Beginnings" (also known as the "Submitted Works Involving Technical Materials and Processes" exhibition) held at the Brooklyn Museum from 1968 to 1969. This exhibition was the culmination of an international competition organized by E.A.T. to foster collaborations between the arts and engineering. Harris's work in this period is documented in the E.A.T. archives at the Getty Research Institute, which holds his submission forms and installation photographs of his work taken by Harry Shunk and János Kender. Additionally, Harris was part of the radical technology movement of the early 1970s; he contributed articles to Spark, the journal of the Committee for Social Responsibility in Engineering (CSRE), including "On Doing It at the Work Place" and "Electronic Control of Deviant, Dissident and Delinquent Americans" (1971), which examined the social and political implications of technological systems.

L. John Harris is a Berkeley-based artist, illustrator, and writer who was active during the late 1960s and 1970s. Born in Los Angeles, he studied art and literature at the University of California, Berkeley, in the 1960s and lived in an art commune in 1968. He became a prominent figure in the California food revolution of the 1970s, famously authoring "The Book of Garlic" (1974) and founding the "Lovers of the Stinking Rose" organization. As an artist and illustrator, he is known for his "Foodoodles" cartoon byline and several illustrated memoirs, including "Café French" (2019) and "Portrait in Red: A Paris Obsession" (2024). While his career as a "bohemian" artist and journalist aligns with the specified time period, his direct affiliation with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is less documented than the collaborative artist John Harris (b. 1937).

results.possibleIdentity: Ultra Violet (September 6, 1935 – June 14, 2014) was the stage name of Isabelle Collin Dufresne, a French-American artist, actress, and author. She was a prominent figure in the New York avant-garde scene of the 1960s and 1970s. Initially a muse and studio assistant to Salvador Dalí in the 1950s, she later became a central member of Andy Warhol's 'Factory' and one of his celebrated 'Superstars' starting in 1964. Her stage name was suggested by Warhol due to her frequent use of the color violet. As an artist, she was involved with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), appearing in their records as a member of the Technical Services Program, which facilitated collaborations between artists and engineers. Her artistic output spanned performance, film, and plastic arts. In the late 1960s and 1970s, she appeared in several underground films, including Warhol's 'I, a Man' and 'Four Stars,' as well as mainstream productions like 'Midnight Cowboy.' Following a near-death experience in 1973, she underwent a religious conversion and later published her memoirs, 'Famous for 15 Minutes: My Years with Andy Warhol,' in 1988. She continued to practice as a visual artist, focusing on themes of light and spirituality, until her death in 2014.

results.possibleIdentity: David Rothenberg (born 1933) is a prominent Broadway producer, publicist, and social activist who was highly active in the New York City arts and political scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Originally a theatrical press agent for figures such as Elizabeth Taylor, Richard Burton, and Edward Albee, he transitioned into producing experimental and socially conscious theater. In 1966, he produced the groundbreaking anti-war rock musical 'Viet Rock' and, in 1967, he produced John Herbert's 'Fortune and Men's Eyes,' a searing drama about homosexuality and sexual slavery in prison. The community of formerly incarcerated people that formed around the play led Rothenberg to found The Fortune Society in 1967, a non-profit dedicated to prisoner rights and reentry support. He was also a civilian observer during the 1971 Attica Prison riot. His historical activity in the NYC experimental theater scene and his role as an 'impresario' of radical art align with the timeframe and creative environment of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement.

David Rothenberg (born 1962) is a philosopher, musician (clarinetist), and professor of philosophy and music at the New Jersey Institute of Technology. While his professional career peak (beginning in the late 1980s and 1990s) is later than the 1960s, he is a primary contemporary figure associated with the themes of 'Experiments in Art and Technology' (E.A.T.). He is the author of 'Hand's End: Technology and the Limits of Nature' (1993), a book that explores technology as an extension of human faculties, and has gained international recognition for his 'interspecies music' collaborations, where he uses technology to perform music with whales, birds, and insects. He is frequently listed in Art and Technology dossiers and retrospectives. He is the son of the poet Jerome Rothenberg, who was active in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1960s.

Donald Rothenberg was a figure active in the late 1960s who occupied the intersection of radical politics and media. He was listed as an Assistant to the Publisher at 'Ramparts' magazine (a publication known for its innovative graphics and radical cultural content) from 1966 through 1967 and served as a chairman for the Spring Mobilization Committee to End the War in Vietnam in 1967. Auction records also exist for a 'Donald Rothenberg' associated with 'Modern Abstract Painting,' suggesting he may have been a visual artist during this period. His presence in the mid-to-late 1960s radical and artistic milieu of the United States makes him a possible match for a 'D. Rothenberg' active at the time.

results.possibleIdentity: John Schaal was an artist and industrial designer active during the 1960s and 1970s. He is noted for his work as a liturgical designer, specifically for creating the black granite altar in the St. John's Chapel at Xavier University in Cincinnati, which was dedicated in 1962. Schaal was also associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, where he was listed as a consulting industrial designer. In addition to his professional work as a designer and artist, he was an art collector; in 2002, he made a bequest of several works by the artist Josef Albers to the Miami University Art Museum (now the Richard and Carole Cocks Art Museum).

results.possibleIdentity: Christopher Tree (often referred to as Chris Tree) is an American avant-garde musician, percussionist, and sound artist who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his solo performance project titled "Spontaneous Sound," which featured a massive collection of over 150 to 200 instruments, including gongs, bells, chimes, timpani, flutes, and a six-foot Tibetan temple horn. Tree's performances were characterized by their lack of traditional structure and emphasis on pure improvisation and environmental interaction. He performed in a wide variety of settings, ranging from high-profile venues like the Cathedral of St. John the Divine in New York City and the Electric Circus to unconventional spaces such as San Quentin Prison and street corners. He was associated with the New York avant-garde and experimental art circles of the era, appearing in a 1968 catalogue for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and being featured in reports from the New York State Council on the Arts (NYSCA). His work was also the subject of a 1967 short documentary film by Les Blank and Pieter Van Deusen titled "Christopher Tree." Notable recordings include his self-released 1970/1971 album "Spontaneous Sound" and a live recording at the Cathedral of St. John the Divine.

results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born May 31, 1931) is a Hungarian-born American artist widely recognized as a pioneer of conceptual, environmental, and ecological art. Often referred to in early professional records and publications as A. Denes, she became active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Her work is characterized by the integration of science, philosophy, linguistics, and technology, a practice she terms "Visual Philosophy." She was significantly involved with the intersection of art and technology during this period; she submitted project proposals to Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) around 1969 and 1970 and was featured in the seminal 1970 exhibition "Software" at the Jewish Museum in New York, which explored information technology's meaning for art. Her early landmark projects include "Rice/Tree/Burial" (1968), considered one of the first site-specific ecological works, and later the iconic "Wheatfield — A Confrontation" (1982). Denes was an early adopter of computer technology in art-making and also experimented with holograms and X-ray imagery in the early 1970s.
